summary: Charity Majors clarifies her stance on Friday deploy freezes, arguing they're a pragmatic workaround rather than a virtue to be celebrated. She offers two practical alternatives: freeze merges instead of deploys to prevent dangerous change buildup, and keep running the deploy pipeline without new code to avoid breakage from prolonged inaction.
key_points: Deploy freezes are a reasonable workaround when teams lack confidence in catching problems before customers do, but shouldn't be treated as a moral stance
Freezing deploys while continuing to merge code creates a dangerous snowdrift of untested changes that explode on January 5th
The real freeze should be on merges, not deploys — merging and deploying should feel like one atomic action
Systems that deploy frequently will likely break if you stop deploying for two weeks due to undetected issues like memory leaks
Keep running the deploy process daily even during quiet periods, just don't ship new code
money_quotes: The one thing that does get my knickers in a twist is when people adopt a holier-than-thou posture towards their Friday deploy freezes. Like they're doing it because they Care About People and it's the Right Thing To Do and some sort of grand moral gesture. Dude, it's a fucking hack. Just admit it.
All these changes build up like a snowdrift over a pile of grenades. You aren't going to find the grenades til you plow into the snowdrift on January 5th, and then you'll find them with your face.
People always remember the outages and instability that we trigger with our actions. We tend to forget about the outages and instability we trigger with our inaction.
If you want to freeze deploys, freeze merges. Let people work on other things. I assure you, there is plenty of other valuable work to be done.
key_insight: Deploy freezes are a practical hack for teams lacking observability confidence, not a virtue — and if you must freeze, freeze merges instead of deploys to avoid a dangerous January pile-up of untested changes.
